text: Most spatiotemporal running variables (contact time, flight time, stride time, duty factor, stride length, swing time) show trivial and non-significant associations with running economy when considered in isolation.
Higher cadence has a small but significant association with better running economy (lower oxygen/energy cost).
Smaller vertical displacement (vertical oscillation) has a significant moderate association with better running economy.
Higher vertical stiffness and leg stiffness show significant moderate associations with better running economy.
Joint angles (ankle, knee, hip) at initial contact, midstance, or toe-off, as well as their range of motion, are not significantly associated with running economy.
Peak vertical ground reaction force, mechanical work variables, and electromyographic activation are not significantly associated with running economy in isolation.
Individual biomechanical variables can explain only 4-12% of between-individual variation in running economy, but combining multiple variables may increase this explanatory power.
